text: The patient is positioned in the modified Lloyd-Davies position for a fully laparoscopic right colectomy. The surgical team places a 10mm Hasson trocar and two 5mm trocars. Extensive omental adhesions from a previous open cholecystectomy are encountered and safely divided using an ultrasonic scalpel. The operative field is exposed by lifting the omentum and the transverse colon.

The surgeon reveals omental infiltration and identifies the ileocolic pedicle, exposing the outlines of the ileocolic vessels and the superior mesenteric artery. Medial to lateral mobilization is performed by opening the peritoneum below the ileocolic vessels, with careful identification and preservation of the duodenum. Dissection proceeds along the root of the mesentery, and the ileocolic vessels are isolated and divided at their origin.

The surgeon divides the coloepiploic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon, followed by a partial omentectomy to excise the infiltrated area. Lateral mobilization of the hepatic flexure, ascending colon, and cecum is performed. The right colic artery is isolated and ligated. The middle colic artery is isolated, with the right branch ligated while sparing the main branch. The mesocolon is divided up to the transverse colon.

The surgeon assesses the blood supply of the transverse colon and terminal ileum using ICG fluorescence before transecting them with a laparoscopic linear stapler. The specimen is retrieved through a Pfannenstiel incision, protected by a wound retractor, and displayed in anterior and posterior views, showing the bulky T4 tumor. An ileocolonic side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, with enterotomies made on the anti-mesenteric side of the ileum and transverse colon. The enterotomy is closed with a double-layer running PDS suture, ensuring the staple line does not include both mesenteries.
